title: NOC 94210 – Furniture and fixture assemblers, finishers, refinishers and inspectors | GTR Immigration
description: In 2026, Canada’s furniture manufacturing industry is blending traditional craftsmanship with high-tech automation to meet the demands of a surging housing market. Furniture and Fixture Assemblers, Finishers, and Inspectors (NOC 94210) are the technical experts who bring interior spaces to life. Whether they are assembling modular office systems, applying eco-friendly "green" lacquers to custom cabinetry, or using digital sensors to inspect structural integrity, these workers are vital to Canada’s $15 billion furniture sector. As the industry prioritizes sustainable materials and local production, skilled finishers and refinishers are seeing a significant increase in demand across Ontario, Quebec, and British Columbia.

classification.teer: FeatureDetailsBroad Occupational Category9 – Occupations in manufacturing and utilitiesTEER Category4 (Usually requiring secondary school and on-the-job training)Major Group94 – Machine operators, assemblers and inspectorsSub-Major Group942 – Assemblers and inspectors in manufacturing

duties: Assembly: Preparing, sanding, and trimming parts to form subassemblies or complete furniture articles using hand and power tools.
Finishing: Setting up and operating finishing machines or finishing furniture by hand with stains, paints, lacquers, or sealers.
Refinishing: Stripping old finishes from used furniture, repairing surfaces, and applying new stains or polishes to restore items to like-new condition.
Inspection: Examining finished furniture and subassemblies for defects, such as scratches, blemishes, or structural weaknesses, ensuring strict quality standards.
2026 Update: Utilizing digital tablets to log "Eco-Impact" data and monitoring automated spray-booth sensors for precise chemical application.
employment_requirements: Education: Completion of secondary school is typically required.
Training: Several weeks of on-the-job training are provided by the employer.
Experience: Experience as a labourer in the same company is often required for advancement to assembler or inspector roles.
Preferred Background: College or industry courses in furniture finishing or refinishing are a significant asset for 2026 roles.
salary_prospects: Note: New federal minimum wage of $18.15/hr effective April 1, 2026, applies to federally regulated employers.
job_outlook: Nationally, the number of job openings is expected to total 6,800, while the number of job seekers is projected at 6,200. This slight gap indicates a shortage risk, particularly for skilled finishers and refinishers. Over 70% of openings will be driven by retirements, making international recruitment a key strategy for Canadian furniture manufacturers through 2033.
labour_market_demand: The 2026 outlook is driven by the National Housing Strategy and the growth of "ready-to-assemble" and custom-built furniture:

Ontario (GTA & Kitchener-Waterloo): Strong demand. This region is Canada's furniture manufacturing heartland. High demand exists for finishers and inspectors in large-scale residential and commercial fixture plants.
Quebec: Very Good prospects. Specifically in Victoriaville and the Beauce region, known for high-end wood furniture and cabinetry exports.
British Columbia: Moderate to Good. Focused on specialized high-end custom furniture and architectural wood products in the Lower Mainland.

National Trend: The Canadian Occupational Projection System (COPS) expects a "Balanced" market nationally, but "Shortages" in specialized finishing and refinishing roles due to a wave of retirements in the trade.
Why Work in Canada in Furniture Manufacturing?
Sustainable Innovation: Work with the latest 2026-grade water-based finishes, UV-curable coatings, and sustainable "bio-composite" materials.
High-Tech Assembly: Gain experience with semi-automated assembly lines and digital quality control software that tracks production from raw wood to finished box.
Path to PR: This NOC is frequently included in Provincial Nominee Program (PNP) lists and is a core qualifier for the 2026 TR to PR transition streams.
Creative Trade Skills: Refinishing roles offer a unique blend of artistry and technical skill, often leading to opportunities for self-employment or specialized restoration work.
pathways: TR to PR Pathway (April 2026): Furniture assemblers and finishers are classified as "Essential Manufacturing Workers," making them eligible for this 33,000-person transition quota.
Ontario Employer Job Offer (In-Demand Skills): This NOC is frequently eligible for provincial nomination for those with a valid job offer in Ontario’s furniture cluster.
Saskatchewan Hard-to-Fill Skills Pilot: A streamlined pathway for technical manufacturing workers to obtain Permanent Residency with a provincial job offer.
BC PNP Entry-Level and Semi-Skilled (ELSS): Provides a direct route for workers in regional BC furniture manufacturing communities.
